# ADMC in Four-Component Form

The realized split introduced in the previous section earns its place only if it maps back to familiar and useful kinematics. It does. Once the directional-positive variables are fixed, the relation between $p_{k^+}$, $p_{k^-}$ and the usual $(M, p_k)$ variables is linear and exactly invertible. As shown in the derivation appendix on the structural uniqueness of the directional-positive split, this realized form is not arbitrary within the natural additive, symmetry-compatible, axis-wise class of two-channel encodings.

## Forward and inverse map

For any chosen direction $k$, the realized directional quantities are
$$
p_{k^+} = M + \tfrac{1}{2} p_k,
\qquad
p_{k^-} = M - \tfrac{1}{2} p_k.
$$

This can be written in matrix form as
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
p_{k^+} \\
p_{k^-}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & \tfrac{1}{2} \\
1 & -\tfrac{1}{2}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
M \\
p_k
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

The inverse relation is
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
M \\
p_k
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} \\
1 & -1
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
p_{k^+} \\
p_{k^-}
\end{pmatrix},
$$
that is,
$$
M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{k^+}+p_{k^-}),
\qquad
p_k = p_{k^+}-p_{k^-}.
$$

So the realized split and the familiar $(M, p_k)$ language are not two disconnected descriptions. Once the working realization is fixed, they are two exactly equivalent ways of organizing the same kinematic content.

### Conservation consequences

Because the map is invertible, conservation in one basis implies conservation in the other. Summing over particles gives
$$
\sum_i M^{(i)} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_i p_{k^+}^{(i)} + \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_i p_{k^-}^{(i)},
$$
and
$$
\sum_i p_k^{(i)} = \sum_i p_{k^+}^{(i)} - \sum_i p_{k^-}^{(i)}.
$$

So if the directional-positive conservation law holds for a chosen direction $k$, then both total $M$ and total $p_k$ are conserved for that direction. In a Cartesian basis this yields conservation of total three-momentum together with conservation of total $M$.

Conversely, if total $M$ and total $p_k$ are conserved, then
$$
\sum_i p_{k^\pm}^{(i)} = \sum_i M^{(i)} \pm \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_i p_k^{(i)}
$$
are conserved as well. At this level, the realized split and the familiar conservation statements are therefore equivalent.

## Relation to standard four-momentum

Once standard energy language is introduced through
$$
E = cM,
$$
the recovered variables align directly with the standard relativistic momentum package. In a Cartesian basis, the quantities
$$
(M, p_x, p_y, p_z)
$$
become, after multiplication of the first component by $c$,
$$
(E/c, p_x, p_y, p_z),
$$
which is the standard four-momentum form.

## SR correspondence review

The inertial relation already established in the previous section is
$$
M = \sqrt{p_f^2 + p^2}.
$$
Together with
$$
p_f = m_0 c
$$
and
$$
E = cM,
$$
this gives
$$
M^2 = p_f^2 + p^2 = m_0^2 c^2 + p^2.
$$
Multiplying by $c^2$ yields
$$
E^2 = m_0^2 c^4 + p^2 c^2,
$$
which is the familiar relativistic energy-momentum relation.

So the present inertial M1 formulation recovers the standard special-relativistic mass-shell structure in momentum-first variables. At this stage, that is the relevant correspondence claim. The section has established an exact invertible map between the directional-positive variables and the familiar $(M, p_k)$ description, equivalence between the corresponding conservation statements, and the recovery of the standard inertial relativistic form.
